title: HTTP/1.1 Message Framing and Length Precedence · Execution Trace: Transfer Encoding Precedence
summary: A recipient reads until EOF for every body, trusts duplicate Content-Length values, accepts Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length, and makes a framing decision before status and method semantics are known. Planned artifact: Add and verify the transfer encoding precedence increment in RFC 9112 message-body length decision table. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Validate transfer-coding order, require final chunked coding for request framing, and close after ambiguous Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length input.
Forbid content framing for HEAD responses, 1xx, 204, successful CONNECT, and other context-specific cases while retaining metadata meaning.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Transfer Encoding Precedence · Theory Transfer Encoding Precedence
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 5276 in a incident replay lab retaining minimized request-smuggling corpora. Build deterministic Go evidence to apply framing precedence and close on Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length ambiguity; preserve this operating constraint: the maintained Go protocol stack remains the production transfer target; then split every structural delimiter across a different read and retain the same result. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Validate transfer-coding order, require final chunked coding for request framing, and close after ambiguous Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length input.
